title: Open Source and Local Code Mode MCP in Deno Sandboxes
author: pmkelly4444
summary:
Replace LLM tool calling with Code Mode in secure sandboxes. Connect AI Agents to the real world with a robust and open source framework.
Get pctx up and running with your preferred package manager
Following Anthropic and Cloudflare's approach to Code Mode, pctx presents MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls, dramatically improving context efficiency and task completion success rates.
An open source framework for code execution with MCP that brings the power of Code Mode to your AI agents
No cloud dependency. Full control over your environment
Works with Claude, GPT, Gemini, or any model you choose
Docker, AWS, GCP, Azure—deploy to any cloud platform
Replace LLM tool calling with Code Mode in secure sandboxes. Connect AI Agents to the real world with a robust and open source framework.
Get pctx up and running with your preferred package manager
Following Anthropic and Cloudflare's approach to Code Mode, pctx presents MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls, dramatically improving context efficiency and task completion success rates.
An open source framework for code execution with MCP that brings the power of Code Mode to your AI agents
No cloud dependency. Full control over your environment
Works with Claude, GPT, Gemini, or any model you choose
Docker, AWS, GCP, Azure—deploy to any cloud platform
Everything you need to know about Code Mode execution with MCP
Code Mode is an approach to AI tool execution where MCP servers are presented as code APIs rather than direct tool calls. Instead of loading all tool definitions upfront and passing large datasets through context, Code Mode enables on-demand tool discovery, processes data in sandboxes, enables parallel execution, and dramatically reduces token usage.
Use pctx when you need better performance, lower costs, or are working with complex multi-tool workflows. pctx reduces token usage by 98.7% (from 150K to 2K tokens), enables parallel execution instead of sequential tool calls, and provides sandbox security. It's especially valuable in production environments where token costs and context limits are concerns.
pctx implements the same Code Mode principles pioneered by Anthropic and Cloudflare: presenting MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls. The key difference is that pctx is open-source (MIT license), self-hostable, and works with any LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.), giving you full control without vendor lock-in.
Absolutely. pctx is designed to work with any LLM (Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, local models, etc.) and integrates with all existing MCP servers. You can connect internal tools, third-party APIs, or custom services. The framework is LLM-agnostic and follows the standard Model Context Protocol, ensuring broad compatibility.
pctx dramatically improves performance. You'll see a 98.7% reduction in token usage (150K → 2K tokens for complex workflows), 5-10x faster execution through parallel processing, and corresponding cost savings. For example, a complex request that costs $0.45 with traditional MCP costs only $0.006 with pctx using Claude Sonnet pricing.
Migration is straightforward. For traditional MCP: install pctx, configure your existing MCP servers, and update prompts to generate code instead of tool calls. For direct APIs: wrap them as MCP servers (many popular services already have MCP implementations) or create simple adapters. Most teams complete migration in a few hours with immediate performance benefits.
No. pctx runs locally on your development machine, in Docker containers, or on any cloud platform (AWS, GCP, Azure, Vercel, Railway, etc.). There's no cloud dependency or required subscription. You have full control over where and how pctx runs, making it ideal for both local development and production deployments.
pctx is built on top of MCP and enhances it with Code Mode execution. While MCP defines how tools communicate with AI systems, pctx optimizes this communication by presenting MCP servers as code APIs, reducing token usage by 98.7%, and enabling sandbox execution. Think of pctx as the production-ready Code Mode framework for MCP.
content:
# Open‑Source Code Mode in Deno Sandboxes: Meet **pctx** *Author: pmkelly4444* | *Score: 9* | [Original post →](https://portofcontext.com) --- ## What is “Code Mode” and why does it matter? When you ask an LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, …) to “use a tool”, the model usually has to: 1. Load the whole tool definition into its prompt. 2. Pass large data blobs back and forth as plain text. 3. Run the calls **sequentially**, one after another. All of that eats tokens, slows down your app, and can quickly become expensive. **Code Mode** flips the script. Instead of sending full tool specs, the LLM receives a **tiny piece of code** that tells a sandboxed server *what* to do. The server (an MCP – Model Context Protocol – endpoint) then executes the code, returns only the result, and the LLM can move on. The result? * **Far fewer tokens** – you only send the code, not the whole tool description. * **Parallelism** – multiple sandboxed tasks can run at the same time. * **Security** – the sandbox isolates each execution, protecting your environment. --- ## Enter **pctx** – an open‑source, MCP‑based framework

pctx takes the ideas pioneered by Anthropic and Cloudflare and packages them into a **self‑hosted, MIT‑licensed** toolkit that works with **any** LLM. No cloud lock‑in, no hidden fees. ### Core features | Feature | Why it’s useful | |---------|-----------------| | **Open source** – MIT license | Full transparency & freedom to modify | | **LLM‑agnostic** – works with Claude, GPT‑4, Gemini, local models | No vendor lock‑in | | **MCP‑first** – presents servers as *code APIs* | Tiny prompts, huge token savings | | **Sandboxed execution** – Docker, Firecracker, or any Deno runtime | Strong security guarantees | | **Parallel processing** – run many tasks at once | 5‑10× speed boost | | **Zero cloud dependency** – run locally or on any cloud (AWS, GCP, Azure, Vercel, Railway…) | Full control over cost & data | --- ## How much can you save? * **Token usage:** ↓ 98.7% (e.g., 150 K → 2 K tokens for a complex workflow) * **Cost:** ↓ ≈ 5 ×  cheaper (a $0.45 Claude Sonnet request drops to $0.006) * **Latency:** ↓ 5‑10× faster thanks to parallel sandbox runs These numbers stack up quickly when you have dozens of daily LLM calls. --- ## Quick‑start: Get pctx running in minutes You can install pctx with the package manager you already use: ```bash # npm / yarn / pnpm npm i -g @portofcontext/pctx # or with Deno deno install -A https://deno.land/x/pctx/cli.ts ``` Then spin up a sandbox: ```bash pctx start --port 8080 # launches a local MCP server in a Docker container ``` Configure your LLM to point to the new endpoint, and replace tool calls in your prompts with **code snippets** that conform to the MCP spec. That’s it – most teams see the performance uplift within a few hours. --- ## Migration checklist (traditional MCP → pctx) 1. **Install pctx** on your dev machine or server. 2. **Add your existing MCP servers** to the `pctx.yaml` config file (just URLs). 3. **Update prompts**: instead of `"call tool X with args Y"`, ask the model to emit JavaScript/TypeScript (or any language the sandbox supports) that performs the action. 4. **Test locally** – pctx will show you the generated code and the sandboxed result. 5. **Deploy** – push the container to your cloud of choice or keep it on‑prem. Most teams finish this loop in a half‑day and instantly enjoy the token and speed gains. --- ## Where can you run pctx? | Environment | How it works | |-------------|--------------| | **Local machine** | Direct Docker or Deno runtime – perfect for development and testing. | | **Docker / Kubernetes** | Deploy as a stateless service behind a load balancer. | | **AWS / GCP / Azure** | Use their container services (ECS, Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps) – no extra code needed. | | **Serverless platforms** (Vercel, Railway) | Deploy the container image; pctx starts the sandbox on demand. | Because the sandbox is isolated, you can safely expose pctx to the internet *only* if you need remote agents to call it. --- ## The big picture: Why you should care - **Cost control** – Token usage is the biggest hidden expense for LLM‑powered products. - **Security** – Sandboxed code execution keeps your data safe from accidental leaks. - **Flexibility** – Works with any model, any tool, any cloud. - **Open source** – No vendor‑specific quirks, you can extend it to fit your own workflow. If you’re building AI agents that need to talk to real‑world services (databases, APIs, file systems), pctx gives you a **robust, production‑ready bridge** without the overhead of traditional tool‑calling. --- ## Ready to give it a spin? 1. Grab the repo: `git clone https://github.com/portofcontext/pctx` 2. Follow the README for step‑by‑step installation. 3. Replace a few tool calls in your existing app with code snippets and watch the tokens shrink. Feel free to drop a comment on the original Hacker News thread if you hit any snags – the community is already experimenting and sharing adapters for popular services. --- ### TL;DR - **Code Mode** = tiny code snippets + sandboxed MCP servers → huge token savings and parallelism. - **pctx** = open‑source, LLM‑agnostic framework that implements Code Mode in Deno/Docker sandboxes. - **Result** = up to 98 % fewer tokens, 5‑10× faster, no cloud lock‑in, full control. Give it a try and see how quickly your AI agents become faster, cheaper, and safer. --- [Source: portofcontext.com](https://portofcontext.com)
